fuzzy_prd:
We need a small toolkit of HTTP payload helpers. First, a way to serialize a deeply nested mapping or ordered list of key-value pairs into a URL-encoded query string — nested keys should use square-bracket notation to preserve structure, and malformed entries (wrong number of elements) should be rejected with a clear error. Second, a user-agent string builder that takes a primary product name and version plus optional extra products and joins them with spaces — reject malformed extras rather than silently dropping them. Third, a multipart form-data serializer that packs several fields (including file-style fields with their own filename, content type, and extra headers) into a properly framed multipart body using a caller-supplied boundary — the output should report the content type (which embeds the boundary) followed by the encoded bytes. Fourth, a multipart decoder that splits a multipart body back into its constituent parts, preserving headers and content for each part — reject non-multipart content types with a clear error naming the offending media type. Also, a single-segment parser that splits headers from body content at the blank-line separator — handle it the same way the decoder handles missing boundaries: reject with a clear neutral error. Finally, a sized streaming reader that wraps an iterator of byte chunks and satisfies exact sized reads, drawing across chunk boundaries.

A couple small clarifications from the questions that came back. For the query helper, if the input can’t actually be understood as a collection of key/value pairs, like a bare scalar or a tuple with the wrong number of elements, the output should be exactly 'error=invalid_query
'. Same idea for the user-agent builder: if the extras are malformed, don’t quietly skip them, return exactly 'error=invalid_extras
'.

For multipart encoding, the first line of multipart_encode output is 'content_type=multipart/form-data; boundary=<boundary>
' followed immediately by the encoded body bytes. The body uses CRLF (
) for all structural newlines. The shape is '--<boundary>
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="<name>"

<value>
' per simple field, closing with '--<boundary>--
'.

On the decode side, the check for whether something is multipart is only about the top-level media type being 'multipart', and that check is case-insensitive. If it isn’t multipart, the output is 'error=not_multipart
mimetype=<media_type>
'. For example 'image/jpeg' produces 'error=not_multipart
mimetype=image/jpeg
'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class MultipartEncoder(fields, boundary=None); class MultipartDecoder(content, content_type, encoding='utf-8'); class StreamingIterator(size, iterator)

trigger_keywords: what is the sized stream read format
how is each read result reported
what is the format of a stream_read output line
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each read produces: 'read=<byte_count_returned>|<bytes_returned>\n'. A null read returns all remaining bytes. A sized read returns exactly that many bytes drawing across chunk boundaries. Once exhausted, every read returns 'read=0|\n'.

trigger_keywords: what error is produced when a body part has no separator
what neutral error for missing CRLF CRLF separator
how is a malformed body segment rejected
how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way the decoder handles missing boundaries: reject with a clear neutral error
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
The BodyPart class raises ImproperBodyPartContentException when the 

 separator is absent. The dispatcher catches this and emits 'error=missing_part_separator
'. Similarly MultipartDecoder raises NonMultipartContentTypeException for non-multipart content types.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. Actions: formdata_urlencode, user_agent, multipart_encode, multipart_decode, bodypart_parse, stream_read.

feature1_1_nested_urlencode.json.description: Serialize an arbitrarily nested mapping or ordered sequence of key/value pairs into a single flat application/x-www-form-urlencoded string. Nested levels are flattened by composing each parent key with the child key wrapped in square brackets, and the whole structure is percent-encoded. Mappings, lists of pairs, and a mix of the two are all accepted and produce equivalent flattened output.

feature1_2_invalid_query.json.description: Reject inputs that cannot be interpreted as a collection of key/value pairs. When a top-level entry is not a two-element pair (for example a bare scalar, or a tuple with the wrong number of elements) the encoder reports a neutral validation error instead of producing output.

feature2_1_build.json.description: Assemble a single user-agent string from a primary product name and version, optionally followed by additional product/version pairs. Each pair is rendered as name slash version, and the pieces are joined by single spaces in the order supplied.

feature2_2_invalid_extras.json.description: Reject extra product entries that are not exact name/version pairs. If any supplied extra entry has more or fewer than two elements, the builder reports a neutral validation error rather than assembling a string.

feature3_multipart_encode.json.description: Serialize a set of form fields into a multipart/form-data body using a caller-supplied boundary. The output reports the resulting content type (which embeds the boundary) followed by the encoded body. Each field becomes a boundary-delimited part with a Content-Disposition header; file-style fields may carry a filename, an explicit content type, and arbitrary extra headers; empty values are preserved.

cases.expected_output:
content_type=multipart/form-data; boundary=this-is-a-boundary
--this-is-a-boundary
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="field"

value
--this-is-a-boundary
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="other_field"

other_value
--this-is-a-boundary--

content_type=multipart/form-data; boundary=this-is-a-boundary
--this-is-a-boundary
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="test"; filename="filename"
Content-Type: application/json

filecontent
--this-is-a-boundary--

feature4_1_decode.json.description: Parse a multipart body into an ordered tuple of parts. The boundary is taken from the content-type parameters (case-insensitively). Each part exposes its headers (preserving order) and its raw content with the leading/trailing boundary framing and the header/body separator removed; a part with no headers yields an empty header set. Content that spans multiple lines is preserved verbatim.

input.content:
--samp1
Header-1: Header-Value-1
Header-2: Header-Value-2

Body 1, Line 1
Body 1, Line 2
--samp1

Body 2, Line 1
--samp1--

cases.expected_output:
parts=2
part0.header.Header-1=Header-Value-1
part0.header.Header-2=Header-Value-2
part0.content=Body 1, Line 1
Body 1, Line 2
part1.content=Body 2, Line 1

feature4_2_non_multipart.json.description: Reject content whose top-level media type is not multipart. When the declared content type does not denote a multipart payload, parsing fails with a neutral error that reports the offending media type.

feature4_3_bodypart.json.description: Parse a single multipart body segment. The segment must contain a blank-line separator between its headers and its content; the content after the separator is returned as text and any headers before it are exposed. A segment that lacks the separator is rejected with a neutral error.

feature5_stream_read.json.description: Stream a sequence of byte chunks of a known total size through a reader that satisfies sized reads. A read with no size returns all remaining source bytes joined together; a sized read returns exactly the requested number of bytes (drawing across chunk boundaries as needed) until the source is exhausted, after which every read returns an empty result. Each output line reports the byte count returned by one read followed by the bytes themselves.

cases.expected_output:
read=17|herearesomechunks
read=0|
read=0|
read=0|
read=0|
read=0|
